title: Acoustic spin resonance in polariton condensates
authors: D. A. Saltykova, A. Kudlis, A. V. Yulin, I. A. Shelykh
abstract: We theoretically investigate acoustic spin resonance in a spatially homogeneous spinor polariton condensate. A longitudinal acoustic wave generates a time-periodic strain-induced effective magnetic field acting on the condensate pseudospin. When this field is transverse to the static in-plane linear-polarization splitting, it resonantly drives polarization oscillations. We show that spin-dependent interactions shift the resonance and produce nonlinear line shapes, while gain, reservoir dynamics, and spin relaxation make the response dissipative and history-dependent, producing amplitude hysteresis. In the presence of lifetime anisotropy, the condensate can develop a bifurcated stationary state with finite circular polarization, and a resonant acoustic drive can switch between the corresponding out-of-plane branches. A Zeeman splitting provides an additional conservative knob for tuning the resonance frequency. Our results identify coherent acoustic driving as a route to resonant, nonlinear, and switchable control of polariton pseudospin dynamics.
summary: This paper theoretically models acoustic spin resonance in polariton condensates, treating the system's pseudospin dynamics using coupled nonlinear equations. It shows that acoustic waves can drive large, nonlinear, and history-dependent oscillations in the condensate's polarization. The findings establish coherent acoustic driving as a powerful, tunable mechanism for controlling quantum spin states in these semiconductor microcavity systems.
main_problem: The paper theoretically investigates acoustic spin resonance in spinor polariton condensates, analyzing how time-periodic strain-induced acoustic fields drive polarization oscillations in the condensate pseudospin.
main_result: The study demonstrates that acoustic driving can induce resonant, nonlinear, and switchable control over the polariton pseudospin dynamics, with lifetime anisotropy enabling the switching between distinct stationary polarization states.
method: The analysis involves deriving and solving coupled, nonlinear differential equations governing the pseudospin and reservoir density, examining both conservative and fully dissipative regimes.
model_or_system: The system is a spinor polariton condensate in a semiconductor microcavity, modeled by an effective pseudospin $\mathbf{S}$ coupled to an excitonic reservoir density $n_R$. The dynamics incorporate static splitting, spin-dependent interactions, and a time-dependent acoustic driving field.
key_observables: Oscillation amplitude ($A_j(\omega)$), out-of-plane pseudospin component ($\langle S_z/|S| angle$), and the resonance frequency ($\omega$).
important_parameters: Static splitting ($\delta$), Zeeman splitting ($\Delta$), spin-dependent interaction ($\alpha_s$), acoustic amplitude ($ eta_h$), and various decay/gain rates ($\gamma, \gamma_a$).
main_assumptions: The analysis employs the single-mode approximation and often linearizes the dynamics around stationary states, though it also explores the full nonlinear, dissipative regime.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the geometry of the resonance, the frequency response showing hysteresis in the dissipative regime, and time-domain traces demonstrating acoustic switching between bifurcated stationary states.
paper_structure: The paper systematically builds the model by deriving the full coupled equations, then analyzes the dynamics in the conservative limit, progresses to the minimal pumped-dissipative regime, and finally explores the effects of lifetime anisotropy and Zeeman tuning to show switchable control.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics and quantum optics as it treats a complex, interacting many-body system (polaritons) using concepts from driven, damped, nonlinear oscillators, showing how external mechanical fields (acoustics) can control quantum coherence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Geometry of acoustic spin resonance in a polariton condensate. A longitudinal acoustic wave prop- agating in the microcavity plane produces a time-periodic strain-induced effective magnetic field acting on the conden- sate pseudospin S. The static field contains the in-plane splitting Bx = −δ and the out-of-plane component Bz = −∆+ αsSz, which combines the Zeeman term and the self- induced field. In the resonant geometry used in the main sim- ulations, ϕ = π/4, the acoustic contribution is transverse to the static in-plane field and is written as By(t) = βh sin(ωt). The acoustic drive can therefore excite pseudospin precession on the Poincar´e sphere and generate large...

(2), with gain, losses, reservoir coupling, lifetime anisotropy, Zeeman split- ting, and spin relaxation neglected. The acoustic wave prop- agates at ϕ = π/4, so that the acoustic field is transverse to the static in-plane splitting. We use E0 = 0.020678 meV, E0/h = 5.00 GHz, t0 = 31.83 ps, and δE = 16.54 µeV. The nonlinear curves are calculated with αs = 0.35, correspond- ing to αphys = 0.905 µeV µm2 and n(c) ∗ = 8.0 µm−2. Figure 4. Bifurcated stationary state and resonant acoustic response induced by lifetime anisotropy. The calculations use ∆= 0, αs = 0.5, δE = 16.54 µeV, and E0 = 0.020678 meV. (a,b) Stationary maps of |⟨Sz/|S|⟩| in the (P/Pth, γa,E) plane without acoustic drive, for λrelax = 0 and λrelax = 0.05, respectively. The white markers indicate the same working point, P/Pth = 1.30 and γa,E = 3.03 µeV. The inset in panel (b) shows the relaxation toward the station- ary out-of-plane state at this working point. (2)–(3) for the minimal pumped–dissipative specializa- tion with γa = ∆= 0, λrelax = 0.05, and ϕ = π/4. The nonlinear curves, maps, and sweeps use αs = 0.5; the blue curve in panel (a) is the linear reference (αs = 0). The di- mensional calibration is E0 = 0.020678 meV, so that E0/h = 5.00 GHz. Spintronics.note: The study models polariton condensates, which are inherently quantum-optical systems, and investigates spin dynamics (pseudospin) driven by external fields (acoustic waves), directly fitting the theme of quantum-optics concepts applied to spin/polariton systems.
